description: Figure 12 displays engineering stress–strain curves for a fully austenitic 0.92C-28.8Mn-9.4Al (wt.%) alloy tested at six austenitic grain sizes: 1.65 μm, 2.2 μm, 8.4 μm, 12.4 μm, 21 μm, and 40 μm, distinguished by solid, dashed, and dotted line styles. The curves demonstrate that decreasing grain size markedly raises both yield and ultimate tensile strengths—the finest 1.65 μm material reaches approximately 980 MPa and fractures near 48% elongation—whereas the coarsest 40 μm specimen exhibits lower strength (~800 MPa) but superior ductility extending beyond 60% elongation. Uniform and total elongation increase with increasing grain size, yet the accompanying text notes a saturation effect above approximately 10 μm. The caption and associated discussion report a classical Hall–Petch relation with coefficients of 461 MPa·μm^1/2 for yield strength and 351 MPa·μm^1/2 for tensile strength. Collectively, this figure evidences the strength–ductility trade-off governed by grain-boundary strengthening in this low-density austenitic steel and identifies a grain-size regime where further coarsening no longer benefits formability.
